title: Thermal expansion of FeWO$_4$ (Ferberite) and FeWO$_4$:Fe$_2$WO$_6$ (7:1): a comparative X-ray and neutron diffraction study
authors: O. Fabelo, L. Cañadillas-Delgado, D. Vie, E. Matesanz, J. Gonzalez-Platas, D. Errandonea
abstract: The thermal expansion of natural FeWO$_4$ (ferberite) and synthetic FeWO$_4$:Fe$_2$WO$_6$ (7:1) was investigated over the 2-1123 K temperature range combining single-crystal and powder X-ray diffraction together with neutron powder diffraction. High-precision lattice parameters were obtained for both samples. The temperature dependence of the unit-cell volume was analysed using physically based thermodynamic models, including the Kroll and Berman approaches as implemented in EoSFit7. All datasets are well reproduced within their respective temperature intervals. However, significant differences are observed between the behavior of ferberite and FeWO$_4$:Fe$_2$WO$_6$, which has a \~40% smaller thermal expansion coefficient and a reduced reference volume. Possible origins, including microstructural and phase-coexistence effects, are discussed. The results provide a comprehensive description of the thermal expansion behavior of FeWO$_4$ across a wide temperature range.
summary: This paper investigates the thermal expansion of ferberite and its synthetic composite over a wide temperature range using X-ray and neutron diffraction. It discovers that the synthetic sample has a much lower thermal expansion coefficient and smaller volume, attributing this to mechanical constraints from a secondary phase. The study provides a comprehensive thermodynamic description of the material's structural evolution.
main_problem: The study aims to characterize the thermal expansion of natural ferberite (FeWO4) and its synthetic composite (FeWO4:Fe2WO6) and to explain the significant discrepancy in their thermal expansion coefficients and unit-cell volumes.
main_result: The synthetic composite exhibits a ~40% smaller thermal expansion coefficient and a reduced reference volume compared to natural ferberite, likely due to mechanical constraints from the Fe2WO6 secondary phase rather than chemical oxidation.
method: The researchers used a multi-technique approach including single-crystal X-ray diffraction, powder X-ray diffraction, and neutron powder diffraction across a temperature range of 2 K to 1123 K.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of natural ferberite (monoclinic wolframite-type structure) and a synthetic 7:1 composite of FeWO4 and Fe2WO6. Thermodynamic modeling was performed using the Kroll and Berman approaches.
key_observables: Lattice parameters (a, b, c, beta), unit-cell volume, thermal expansion coefficient, and Fe-O bond distances.
important_parameters: Temperature range (2-1123 K), Neel temperature (T_N approx 62 K), and the 7:1 phase ratio in the synthetic sample.
main_assumptions: The authors assume that the observed volume contraction is not due to Fe2+ to Fe3+ oxidation or oxygen vacancies, as evidenced by the stability of Fe-O bond distances.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the ferberite single crystal; Figure 2 and 4 show temperature evolution of unit-cell volume; Figure 3 shows neutron diffraction Rietveld refinement and magnetic Bragg reflections; Figure 5 shows anisotropic lattice parameter evolution; Figure 6 shows high-temperature volume expansion trends.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of thermal expansion discrepancy, describes the experimental setup (XRD and Neutron diffraction), presents the detailed temperature-dependent structural data, discusses the thermodynamic modeling results, and concludes with a mechanistic explanation for the observed differences between natural and synthetic samples.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. View of the single crystal of ferberite used in the SC-XRD experiment in the Bruker D8 diffractometer.

Figure 2. Temperature evolution of the unit-cell volume for the ferberite sample determined by X-ray diffraction. Single-crystal measurements (circles) were collected between 40 and 500 K, while powder X-ray diffraction measurements on the corresponding ground sample (squares) were

Figure 3. (Top) Rietveld reﬁnement of the neutron powder diffraction pattern of synthetic FeWO₄ measured at D20 at 300 K (λ = 1.54119(3) Å). The experimental data and calculated proﬁle are shown as red and black lines, respectively, while the blue curve represents the difference. Vertical green, orange, and red ticks indicate the Bragg reﬂection positions of the different phases. The second phase corresponds to V from the sample holder, whereas the third phase is attributed to Fe₂WO₆, reﬁned in the orthorhombic Pbcn space group with lattice parameters a = 4.6000(6), b = 16.814(3), and c = 4.9674(7) Å. This contribution was modeled using a Le Bail approach, leading to a ﬁnal χ² = 1.26....

Figure 4. Results from the experiments performed in synthetic FeWO4. Volume behavior between 2 and 470 K measured using neutrons in D20 diffractometer at ILL (circles) and 15-473 K results obtained from powder XRD (squares). Error bars are smaller than symbols. The lines show the ﬁt of the data to Kroll model.

Figure 5: (Top) Temperature evolution of the lattice parameters of ferberite derived from single- crystal and powder diffraction measurements (open and ﬁlled symbols, respectively). (Bottom) Temperature evolution of the lattice parameters of synthesized FeWO₄ obtained from powder diffraction data.
